docs.title: CircuitTikZ Visualizer
docs.text: A Python desktop application for building circuit diagrams visually and generating LaTeX CircuitTikZ code. Place components on a grid canvas, connect them with wires, and render the result as a PDF preview, all without writing LaTeX by hand.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Visual drag-and-drop editor — place components on a snap-to-grid canvas with two-click wiring
  • Live LaTeX preview — renders the circuit through pdflatex and displays the PDF inline
  • Manual LaTeX tab — write CircuitTikZ code directly and render it
  • Convention selector — switch between European (IEC) and American (IEEE/ANSI) symbol styles
  • Save / Load — circuits stored as JSON files (Ctrl+S / Ctrl+O)
  • Export .tex — export a complete standalone LaTeX document
  • Undo / Redo — Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Shift+Z with full state snapshots
  • Property panel — edit label, value, current arrow (i=), and annotation (a=) per component

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.13+
  • uv — fast Python package manager
  • MiKTeX or TeX Live (provides pdflatex)

docs.title: 1. Clone the repository
docs.text: git clone <repo-url>

docs.title: 2. Create a virtual environment and install dependencies
docs.text:
uv venv
uv pip install PyQt5 PyMuPDF

docs.title: 3. Activate the virtual environment
docs.text:
# Windows PowerShell
.\.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1

# Windows CMD
.\.venv\Scripts\activate.bat

### 4. Install LaTeX dependencies

The app needs `pdflatex` with the following LaTeX packages: **circuitikz**, **standalone**, and **siunitx**.

**Windows (MiKTeX):**
```powershell
winget install MiKTeX.MiKTeX --accept-package-agreements --accept-source-agreements
miktex packages install circuitikz standalone siunitx

docs.title: 5. Run
docs.text: python main.py

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
1. Place components — click a component in the left palette, then click on the grid to place it (two clicks for two-terminal components)
  2. Wire mode — select "Wire" from the palette, click to add waypoints, double-click or press Escape to finish
  3. Select — click near a component to select it; right-click or Escape to deselect
  4. Edit properties — use the property panel (bottom-left) to change type, label, value, current arrow, or annotation
  5. Drag — click and drag endpoints or the body of a selected component to reposition it
  6. Delete — press Delete or use the "Delete Selected" button
  7. Render — press Ctrl+Enter or click "Render" to compile and preview the LaTeX output
  8. Convention — use the dropdown in the toolbar to switch between European/American symbol styles

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
main.py       Entry point, MainWindow, LaTeX compilation pipeline
models.py     Component, Busbar, Circuit dataclasses + COMPONENTS registry
latex.py      LaTeX code generation + JSON save/load serialization
scene.py      CircuitScene (QGraphicsScene) + CircuitCanvas (QGraphicsView)
panels.py     ComponentPalette + PropertyPanel widgets

docs.title: Canvas Architecture — scene.py
docs.text: This document explains how the visual circuit editor canvas is implemented.

docs.title: Two Classes
docs.text:
The canvas is built with Qt's Graphics View Framework using two classes:

Class Base Class Role CircuitScene QGraphicsScene Owns the data, handles all logic (placement, selection, drag, draw) CircuitCanvas QGraphicsView Thin viewport — zoom, mouse tracking, key forwarding

CircuitCanvas delegates almost everything to CircuitScene. It only handles: - Mouse wheel → zoom (scale by 1.15×) - Mouse move → forwards to scene.update_preview() or scene.drag_move() - Escape / Delete keys - zoom_to_fit() — computes bounding rect of all components and calls fitInView()

All the real work happens in CircuitScene.

docs.title: Grid System
docs.text:
• Grid size: 50 px per unit (GRID_SIZE = 50)
  • Grid extent: ±15 units → 31×31 grid = 961 dot positions
  • Grid dots are drawn once in _init_grid() as tiny ellipses at z-level -10
  • All component coordinates are stored in grid units (integers), not pixels
  • Pixel conversion: px = gx * GRID_SIZE, py = gy * GRID_SIZE

docs.title: Snapping
docs.text: _snap(pos) converts a raw mouse position to the nearest grid point: 1. Round to nearest integer grid coordinates 2. If in placement or wiring mode, also check all pin offsets of existing components 3. If a pin is within 1.5 grid units, snap to that pin instead

docs.title: State Machine (Modes)
docs.text:
The scene operates as a state machine with these modes:

idle ──────────────┬──► place_single ──► idle     (one-click: grounds, nodes)
                   ├──► place_first ──► place_second ──► idle   (two-click: R, C, L, etc.)
                   ├──► busbar_first ──► busbar_second ──► idle
                   └──► wire ──► wire ──► ... ──► idle   (multi-click, double-click to finish)
  • idle: click to select, right-click to deselect, drag selected component
  • place_single: one click places a node-style component (ground, VCC, transistor, op-amp)
  • place_first / place_second: two clicks define start and end of a two-terminal component
  • busbar_first / busbar_second: two clicks define a busbar span
  • wire: each click adds a waypoint; consecutive pairs become short components; double-click or Escape finishes

Mode transitions happen via _set_mode(), which also fires the on_mode_changed callback so MainWindow can update the status bar text.

docs.title: mousePressEvent (left click)
docs.text: Depending on current mode: - idle: try to start a drag on the selected component, or call _try_select() to find the nearest component - place_single: add component, return to idle - place_first: store first position, move to place_second - place_second: add two-terminal component between first and second click, return to idle - wire: append waypoint, create a short segment between last two waypoints

docs.title: mousePressEvent (right click)
docs.text: Cancels the current placement mode or deselects.

docs.title: mouseDoubleClickEvent
docs.text: In wire mode, finishes the wire and returns to idle.

docs.title: mouseMoveEvent
docs.text:
• If dragging: calls drag_move() to reposition the drag target
  • Otherwise: calls update_preview() to show a crosshair at the snapped position and a dashed line from the first click point

docs.title: Selection
docs.text:
_try_select(gx, gy) finds the closest component or busbar: - Node-style components: distance = squared distance to (x1, y1) - Two-terminal components: distance = point-to-segment distance (using projection formula in _point_to_segment_dist2) - Busbars: same point-to-segment distance - Selects the nearest object if distance ≤ 2.5 grid units; otherwise deselects

Selection fires on_selection_changed so the property panel updates.

docs.title: Dragging
docs.text:
When clicking on an already-selected component in idle mode:

  1. _drag_hit() checks what part was clicked:
  2. Near (x1, y1) → drag "start" endpoint
  3. Near (x2, y2) → drag "end" endpoint
  4. Near the body (line segment) → drag "body" (moves both endpoints)
  5. Node-style components → always drag as "body"
  6. drag_move() updates the component coordinates to the new snapped position on each mouse move
  7. mouseReleaseEvent ends the drag

Busbars have similar logic via _busbar_drag_hit() (start/end/body).

docs.title: Drawing (_redraw)
docs.text:
Every time the circuit changes, _redraw() is called:

  1. Removes all items in _dynamic_items from the scene
  2. Redraws all busbars (_draw_busbar)
  3. Redraws all components (_draw_comp)
  4. Draws connection node dots (_draw_nodes)
  5. Fires on_circuit_changed callback (triggers LaTeX regeneration)

docs.title: Component Drawing (_draw_comp)
docs.text:
Components are drawn differently based on their type:

Category Visual Representation short (wire) Simple line in black/orange Two-terminal (R, C, L, etc.) Line with a labeled box at midpoint Ground variants Vertical line + horizontal bars (decreasing width) VCC / VEE Vertical line + horizontal bar Transistors Rectangle with type name Op-amps Triangle with +/− labels Logic gates, ICs Labeled rectangle (size varies by category) Transformers Two side-by-side circles (coils) Tubes Circle with label Generator / Motor Circle with G or M letter

Colors: - Selected → orange (255, 140, 0) - Custom color → from comp.color - Default → blue (0, 60, 180) - Wires → black when unselected, orange when selected

docs.title: Annotations on Two-Terminal Components
docs.text:
Three annotation layers are drawn alongside the component line:

  1. Voltage (red): value text + polarity symbols (+/−), positioned perpendicular to the line. Direction controlled by voltage_dir (v=, v<=, v^=, v_=).
  2. Current (green): arrow + label, positioned below or above. Direction controlled by current_dir (i=, i<=, i^=, i_=).
  3. Flow (blue): arrow + label, similar to current. Direction controlled by flow_dir (f=, f<=, f^=, f_=).

docs.title: Node Dots (_draw_nodes)
docs.text: Black filled dots (6px diameter, z-level 4) at every endpoint and pin position, indicating connection points.

docs.title: Pin Offsets
docs.text: Multi-port components (transistors, op-amps, ICs) define pin_offsets in the COMPONENTS registry — relative grid offsets for each named pin. These are drawn as small gray dots with labels, and the snapping system uses them for precise wiring.

docs.title: Preview System
docs.text: update_preview() draws temporary visual aids (stored in _preview_items, cleared each frame): - Orange dashed crosshair at the current snapped position - Green highlight circle when snapped to a pin - Dashed line from first click point (during two-click placement) - Dashed line from last wire waypoint (during wire mode)

docs.title: Undo / Redo
docs.text:
• _snapshot() serializes the entire Circuit to a dict (via circuit_to_dict) and pushes it onto _undo_stack. Clears _redo_stack.
  • undo() pushes current state to redo stack, pops from undo stack, deserializes
  • redo() reverses the process
  • Snapshots are taken before any mutation (place, delete, drag start, property change)

docs.title: Z-Level Ordering
docs.text: Z-Level Content -10 Grid dots 1 Lines (wires, busbars, component lines) 2 Shapes (rectangles, ellipses, polygons, label backgrounds) 3 Text labels, annotations, pin labels 4 Connection node dots 5 Pin snap highlights

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This application lets users build circuit diagrams visually (or write LaTeX manually) and renders them as PDF previews. The pipeline goes from GUI interaction → Python data model → LaTeX code generation → pdflatex compilation → PDF → pixel display.

docs.title: 1. User Input — scene.py, panels.py
docs.text:
• The user places components on a grid canvas (CircuitScene / CircuitCanvas) or writes LaTeX directly in the manual editor tab.
  • Component palette (ComponentPalette in panels.py) lists all available components grouped by category.
  • Property panel (PropertyPanel) allows editing label, value, current, annotation, and color per component.
  • All interactions result in changes to the in-memory Circuit object.

docs.title: 2. Data Model — models.py
docs.text:
• Component dataclass: stores kind, label, value, coordinates (x1, y1, x2, y2), current, annotation, color, etc.
  • Busbar dataclass: stores label, y-position, and x-range.
  • Circuit dataclass: holds lists of Component and Busbar objects.
  • COMPONENTS dict: registry mapping component keys (e.g. "R", "C", "trafo") to their CircuitTikZ names, categories, and rendering metadata.

docs.title: 3. LaTeX Code Generation — latex.py
docs.text:
• generate_latex(circuit) iterates over all busbars and components in the Circuit and produces a \begin{circuitikz}...\end{circuitikz} snippet.
  • Each component is mapped to its CircuitTikZ \draw ... to[<type>, <options>] ... command using the COMPONENTS registry.
  • Custom colors are converted to \definecolor statements.
  • Node-style components (grounds, power symbols, transistors) use \node[<style>] instead of \draw ... to[...].

docs.title: 4. LaTeX Wrapping — main.py → latex_template()
docs.text:
• The snippet is wrapped in a full standalone LaTeX document: latex \documentclass[border=10pt]{standalone} \usepackage[<convention>, siunitx]{circuitikz} \usepackage{xcolor} \begin{document} <snippet> \end{document}
  • The convention (European/American) is selected by the user via a dropdown and passed to the circuitikz package option.

docs.title: 5. Compilation via pdflatex — main.py → compile_latex()
docs.text:
• This is where MiKTeX is involved.
  • find_pdflatex() locates the pdflatex executable:
  • Checks hardcoded MiKTeX paths (AppData/Local/Programs/MiKTeX/... and C:/Program Files/MiKTeX/...)
  • Falls back to where.exe pdflatex (Windows) or which pdflatex (Linux/macOS)
  • compile_latex():
  • Creates a temporary directory
  • Writes the full LaTeX document to circuit.tex
  • Calls pdflatex -interaction=nonstopmode -output-directory=<tmp> circuit.tex
  • If circuit.pdf is produced → success; otherwise returns the pdflatex error log

docs.title: 6. PDF to Pixel Display — main.py → pdf_to_pixmap()
docs.text:
• Uses PyMuPDF (fitz) to open the compiled PDF.
  • Renders the first page at 200 DPI to a pixel buffer.
  • Converts the buffer to a QPixmap and displays it in a QLabel inside a scroll area.

docs.title: 7. Cleanup
docs.text: • On window close, all temporary directories (containing .tex, .pdf, .aux, .log files) are deleted via shutil.rmtree.

docs.title: How MiKTeX Fits In
docs.text:
MiKTeX provides the pdflatex compiler, which is the core of the rendering pipeline. Without it, the app can still generate LaTeX code but cannot render previews.

Required LaTeX packages (must be installed in MiKTeX):

Package Purpose circuitikz Provides all circuit component macros standalone Produces a tightly-cropped PDF (no margins) siunitx Proper formatting of SI units in labels xcolor Custom color support for components

How MiKTeX is called:

pdflatex -interaction=nonstopmode -output-directory=<tmpdir> <tmpdir>/circuit.tex
  • -interaction=nonstopmode prevents pdflatex from stopping on errors and waiting for user input.
  • The output PDF is read back by PyMuPDF immediately after compilation.
  • MiKTeX's auto-install feature may download missing packages on first use if enabled.

docs.title: File Responsibilities
docs.text: File Role main.py Entry point, MainWindow, LaTeX template, compilation pipeline (find_pdflatex, compile_latex, pdf_to_pixmap) models.py Data model (Component, Busbar, Circuit) and COMPONENTS registry latex.py LaTeX code generation from Circuit + JSON save/load scene.py Canvas interaction (placement, wiring, selection, drag, undo/redo) panels.py Component palette tree and property editor panel

docs.title: Data Flow Summary
docs.text:
User clicks grid          → Component added to Circuit (models.py)
Circuit changes            → generate_latex() produces LaTeX snippet (latex.py)
Auto-build or Ctrl+Enter   → compile_latex() writes .tex, calls pdflatex (MiKTeX)
pdflatex produces .pdf     → pdf_to_pixmap() renders it via PyMuPDF (fitz)
QPixmap displayed           → User sees the rendered circuit
